bytes.topic: Batch vs Streaming — when to process in batches vs real-time, trade-offs
bytes.title: Spark Batch vs Streaming — Choosing the Right Execution Model
bytes.body:
Spark gives you a unified DataFrame API for both modes, but the execution model underneath is fundamentally different. Batch jobs with `spark.read` load a bounded dataset, optimize the full DAG via Catalyst, and maximize throughput — ideal for nightly ETL, backfills, or ML training pipelines where latency is irrelevant. Structured Streaming with `spark.readStream` treats an unbounded source as an infinite table, emitting results per micro-batch or (experimentally) continuously.

The key trade-off is latency vs. throughput. Batch achieves higher throughput because Spark can sort-merge shuffle data globally. Streaming micro-batches cap at the trigger interval — `Trigger.ProcessingTime("1 minute")` — so end-to-end latency is bounded but shuffle scope is limited to each batch window. `Trigger.Once()` bridges both worlds: streaming semantics with a single batch execution, useful for incremental loads without a persistent streaming job.

Checkpointing overhead is streaming-only — Spark writes offsets and state to a checkpoint location on every trigger, which adds I/O cost absent in batch. If your SLA tolerates 15+ minute latency, batch wins on cost and simplicity every time.
bytes.code_snippet:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import window, count

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("batch-vs-stream").getOrCreate()

# Batch: bounded read, full Catalyst optimization
batch_df = spark.read.parquet("s3://events/2024/")
batch_df.groupBy("user_id").count().write.parquet("s3://output/batch/")

# Streaming: unbounded, micro-batch per trigger
stream_df = spark.readStream.format("kafka") \
    .option("subscribe", "events") \
    .load()

query = stream_df.groupBy(window("timestamp", "5 minutes"), "user_id") \
    .agg(count("*").alias("event_count")) \
    .writeStream \
    .trigger(processingTime="1 minute") \
    .option("checkpointLocation", "s3://checkpoints/events/") \
    .outputMode("update") \
    .format("delta") \
    .start()

bytes.topic: Apache Spark — RDDs vs DataFrames, transformations vs actions, lazy evaluation
bytes.title: Spark RDDs vs DataFrames — Lazy Evaluation, Transformations, and When Each Fires
bytes.body:
Spark's execution model revolves on a hard boundary: transformations are lazy, actions trigger computation. Every `.map()`, `.filter()`, `.select()`, or `.join()` builds a DAG — nothing runs. The moment you call `.count()`, `.collect()`, `.write()`, or `.show()`, Spark submits stages to executors.

RDDs give you row-level control with typed lambda functions but bypass Catalyst optimizer and Tungsten encoder — you pay with slower execution and manual serialization. DataFrames push predicates down, prune columns early, and generate optimized physical plans via Catalyst. For most pipelines, DataFrames outperform equivalent RDD code by 2–5x without tuning.

Where RDDs still win: custom partitioning logic, processing unstructured binary data, or when you need `mapPartitionsWithIndex()` for partition-aware side effects. Converting between them is cheap — `df.rdd` or `spark.createDataFrame(rdd, schema)` — but crossing the boundary drops Catalyst benefits.

Key rule: never call `.collect()` on large datasets. It pulls all partitions to the driver heap. Prefer `.write.parquet()` or `.foreach()` for distributed output.
bytes.code_snippet:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("demo").getOrCreate()

# DataFrame — Catalyst optimizes this full chain before any execution
df = (
    spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/events/")
    .filter(col("event_type") == "click")   # transformation — lazy
    .select("user_id", "timestamp", "page") # transformation — lazy
    .groupBy("user_id")
    .count()                                 # transformation — lazy
)

# Action — DAG submits here
df.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("s3://bucket/click_counts/")

# Equivalent RDD — no Catalyst, manual schema, slower
rdd = spark.sparkContext.textFile("s3://bucket/raw/")
click_counts = (
    rdd.filter(lambda line: "click" in line)  # lazy
       .map(lambda line: (line.split(",")[0], 1))  # lazy
       .reduceByKey(lambda a, b: a + b)            # lazy
)
click_counts.saveAsTextFile("s3://bucket/rdd_out/")  # action

bytes.topic: Apache Flink — event time vs processing time, watermarks, stateful streaming
bytes.title: Spark Structured Streaming — Event Time, Watermarks, and Stateful Ops
bytes.body:
Spark Structured Streaming distinguishes between event time (the timestamp embedded in the event payload) and processing time (when Spark ingests the record). For late-arriving data — common with mobile clients or distributed producers — processing time is meaningless; only event time reflects what actually happened.

`withWatermark("eventTime", "10 minutes")` tells Spark's internal state store how long to hold open a time window before declaring it complete. Once the max observed event time minus the watermark threshold crosses a window boundary, Spark emits the aggregate and purges that window's state. Without a watermark, state grows unboundedly across micro-batches.

Stateful ops like `mapGroupsWithState` and `flatMapGroupsWithState` let you maintain arbitrary per-key state across batches. The `GroupState` API gives explicit control — check timeouts, update accumulators, and decide when to emit downstream. For windowed aggregations, prefer `window()` + `withWatermark()`: Spark's planner can optimize these into RocksDB-backed state stores, bounding memory even under high-cardinality keys.
bytes.code_snippet:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import window, col

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("event-time-demo").getOrCreate()

events = (
    spark.readStream
    .format("kafka")
    .option("subscribe", "clicks")
    .load()
    .selectExpr("CAST(value AS STRING) AS data", "timestamp")
)

result = (
    events
    .withWatermark("timestamp", "10 minutes")
    .groupBy(window(col("timestamp"), "5 minutes", "1 minute"))
    .count()
)

result.writeStream.outputMode("update").format("console").start().awaitTermination()

bytes.topic: dbt — model types (table/view/incremental/snapshot), tests, macros, lineage
bytes.title: dbt + Spark — Model Types, Tests, and Lineage with the dbt-spark Adapter
bytes.body:
dbt-spark exposes four materializations, each with Spark-specific behavior. `view` creates a Spark SQL view — cheap but recomputed on every query. `table` runs a full `CREATE TABLE AS SELECT` using your configured `file_format` (parquet, delta, iceberg). `incremental` is where Spark diverges: with Delta Lake, set `incremental_strategy='merge'` and a `unique_key`; without it, fall back to `insert_overwrite` on `partition_by` columns — dbt rewrites only touched partitions. `snapshot` uses `dbt_valid_from`/`dbt_valid_to` SCD Type 2 logic, stored as a Delta table for time-travel support.

Tests live in schema.yml — `not_null`, `unique`, `accepted_values`, `relationships`. Add `dbt test --select +my_model` to run upstream tests too. Macros are Jinja2 functions: use `{{ ref('model') }}` to wire lineage (dbt builds the DAG from these calls) and `{{ source('schema', 'table') }}` to track raw ingestion tables. Run `dbt docs generate && dbt docs serve` to visualize the full Spark pipeline graph, including column-level lineage with dbt 1.6+ and a supported metadata adapter.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- models/events_incremental.sql
{{ config(
    materialized='incremental',
    file_format='delta',
    incremental_strategy='merge',
    unique_key='event_id',
    partition_by=['event_date']
) }}

SELECT
    event_id,
    user_id,
    cast(event_ts AS DATE) AS event_date,
    event_ts
FROM {{ source('raw', 'events') }}
{% if is_incremental() %}
WHERE event_ts > (SELECT MAX(event_ts) FROM {{ this }})
{% endif %}

bytes.topic: Apache Airflow — DAGs, operators, sensors, XCom, scheduling
bytes.title: Spark + Airflow — Wiring SparkSubmitOperator, Sensors, and XCom Into Real Pipelines
bytes.body:
Airflow orchestrates Spark jobs via `SparkSubmitOperator` from `apache-airflow-providers-apache-spark`. Each task wraps a `spark-submit` call — you inject executor configs (`spark.executor.memory`, `spark.dynamicAllocation.enabled`) and pass runtime args using Jinja templating (`{{ ds }}` for execution date) directly into `application_args`.

Sensors gate your Spark jobs on upstream data. Use `S3KeySensor` to block submission until the expected partition lands (`events/dt={{ ds }}/`), or `SparkSqlSensor` to poll until a Hive/Spark SQL query returns rows. Without sensors, SparkSubmitOperator blindly reads empty partitions and silently produces bad output.

`SparkSubmitOperator` doesn't natively push XCom values. To pass a Spark job's output path downstream, wrap the submission in a `PythonOperator` that calls `spark-submit` via subprocess, captures the application ID, and calls `ti.xcom_push(key='app_id', value=app_id)`. Downstream tasks pull it with `{{ ti.xcom_pull(task_ids='run_etl', key='app_id') }}`.

Set `schedule="0 2 * * *"` and always `catchup=False` in production. Airflow backfilling missed Spark runs across 90 days will saturate your cluster's executor pool and starve live jobs.
bytes.code_snippet:
from airflow import DAG
from airflow.providers.apache.spark.operators.spark_submit import SparkSubmitOperator
from airflow.sensors.s3_key_sensor import S3KeySensor
from datetime import datetime

with DAG("spark_daily_etl", start_date=datetime(2024, 1, 1), schedule="0 2 * * *", catchup=False) as dag:
    gate = S3KeySensor(
        task_id="wait_partition", bucket_name="data-lake", bucket_key="events/dt={{ ds }}/"
    )
    etl = SparkSubmitOperator(
        task_id="run_etl",
        application="/jobs/etl.py",
        conn_id="spark_default",
        conf={"spark.executor.memory": "4g", "spark.dynamicAllocation.enabled": "true"},
        application_args=["--date", "{{ ds }}"],
    )
    gate >> etl

bytes.topic: Prefect — flows, tasks, deployments, work pools, modern Python-native orchestration
bytes.title: Spark + Prefect — Orchestrating PySpark Pipelines with Flows, Tasks, and Work Pools
bytes.body:
Prefect wraps PySpark pipelines as observable, retryable flows without Airflow's DAG boilerplate. Each `@task` becomes an independently tracked unit in the Prefect UI — ingest, transform, write — with per-task retry logic and live state visibility.

The critical pattern: pass storage paths between tasks, not DataFrames. Spark DataFrames are not serializable across Prefect's result backend, so each task reads from and writes to a shared path (S3, ADLS, GCS). This aligns naturally with Spark's lazy evaluation model — each task forces materialization before the next stage reads it, giving you clean stage boundaries.

For retries, annotate heavy tasks with `retries=2, retry_delay_seconds=30`. Prefect re-executes only the failed stage, not the full Spark job — critical when upstream stages are expensive shuffles or large aggregations.

Deploy with `prefect deploy` to a work pool. Use a `Process` pool for local Spark clusters or a `Kubernetes` pool for spark-on-k8s. Flow `parameters` let you vary `raw_path`, executor cores, or driver memory per run without touching business logic — keeping SparkSession config as deployment-time concerns.
bytes.code_snippet:
from prefect import flow, task
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql import functions as F

@task(retries=2, retry_delay_seconds=30, name="ingest-raw-events")
def ingest(spark: SparkSession, raw_path: str) -> str:
    staging = raw_path.replace("raw", "staging")
    spark.read.parquet(raw_path).write.mode("overwrite").parquet(staging)
    return staging

@task(name="aggregate-by-user")
def aggregate(spark: SparkSession, staging_path: str) -> str:
    curated = staging_path.replace("staging", "curated")
    (spark.read.parquet(staging_path)
         .groupBy("user_id")
         .agg(F.count("event_id").alias("event_count"))
         .write.mode("overwrite").parquet(curated))
    return curated

@flow(log_prints=True, name="spark-event-pipeline")
def event_pipeline(raw_path: str, app_name: str = "prefect-spark"):
    spark = SparkSession.builder.appName(app_name).getOrCreate()
    staging = ingest(spark, raw_path)
    curated = aggregate(spark, staging)
    print(f"Done: {curated}")

bytes.topic: Dagster — assets, jobs, schedules, sensors, Software-Defined Assets
bytes.title: Spark + Dagster Software-Defined Assets — Orchestrate DataFrames, Not Tasks
bytes.body:
Dagster's Software-Defined Assets flip the orchestration model: instead of defining tasks that execute code, you declare data assets and Dagster infers the DAG from dependencies. For Spark engineers, this maps directly onto your DataFrame pipeline — each `@asset` represents a logical dataset, and lineage is tracked automatically.

Wire a `SparkSession` through a Dagster resource so every asset shares a pre-configured session without re-initialization per step. Dependencies are expressed as function arguments: `cleaned_events(raw_events: DataFrame)` tells Dagster `cleaned_events` depends on `raw_events` — no explicit edges needed.

Jobs group asset selections for execution. `ScheduleDefinition` runs jobs on a cron cadence. Sensors let you trigger materializations reactively — fire `events_pipeline` when a new Parquet partition lands in S3 via `S3GlobSensorDefinition`.

The critical shift from Airflow: assets represent *what* data exists, not *how* to compute it. Dagster's asset catalog exposes materialization history, freshness, and downstream impact — essential when a Spark job rewrites a shared Delta table that feeds 10 downstream assets. Partition-aware assets let Dagster materialize only the missing date partitions, avoiding full Spark re-scans.

bytes.topic: Pipeline Design — idempotency, retry logic, backfill strategy
bytes.title: Spark Pipeline Design — Idempotency, Retry Logic, and Backfill Strategy
bytes.body: Spark pipelines fail. Design for it upfront. Idempotency means re-running a job for the same partition produces identical output — achieve this by writing to a staging path then atomically renaming, or by using `insertOverwrite` on Hive/Delta partitions rather than append. With Delta Lake, `replaceWhere` scopes overwrites to a predicate, making reruns safe: `df.write.format("delta").option("replaceWhere", "date = '2024-01-15'").mode("overwrite").save(path)`. For retry logic, wrap stage transitions in try/except and checkpoint intermediate DataFrames to HDFS/S3 using `df.write.parquet(checkpointPath)` — on retry, detect the checkpoint and skip upstream recomputation. Avoid relying solely on Spark's internal task retries (`spark.task.maxFailures`); those retry at the task level, not the stage level, so corrupt source data will keep failing. Backfill strategy: parameterize your job on `start_date`/`end_date`, partition your output by date, and drive backfill via a loop in your orchestrator (Airflow, Dagster). Never hardcode dates inside the Spark job itself. Use `spark.read.option("basePath", root).parquet(f"{root}/date={dt}")` to read specific partitions efficiently without full scans.
bytes.code_snippet:
from delta.tables import DeltaTable

def run_pipeline(spark, processing_date: str, output_path: str):
    checkpoint = f"{output_path}/_checkpoint/{processing_date}"

    # Skip recomputation if checkpoint exists
    if DeltaTable.isDeltaTable(spark, checkpoint):
        df = spark.read.format("delta").load(checkpoint)
    else:
        df = spark.read.parquet(f"s3://raw/date={processing_date}")
        df = df.transform(enrich).transform(aggregate)
        df.write.format("delta").mode("overwrite").save(checkpoint)

    # Idempotent write — scoped overwrite, safe to retry
    df.write.format("delta") \
        .option("replaceWhere", f"date = '{processing_date}'") \
        .mode("overwrite") \
        .save(output_path)

bytes.topic: Data Partitioning — processing in chunks, partition pruning, parallelism
bytes.title: Spark Data Partitioning — Pruning, Parallelism, and Processing in Chunks
bytes.body:
Spark processes data in partitions — the fundamental unit of parallelism. Each partition maps to one task on one executor core. Too few partitions starve your cluster; too many cause scheduling overhead. The sweet spot is typically 2–4x your total executor cores.

Partition pruning is Spark's ability to skip irrelevant partitions entirely at the file level. When your DataFrame is backed by Parquet partitioned by `event_date`, a filter like `df.filter(col("event_date") == "2024-01-15")` causes Spark to read only that directory — the Catalyst optimizer pushes the predicate down before any data is loaded. Verify this in the physical plan: look for `PartitionFilters` in the output of `df.explain(true)`.

For wide transformations like `groupBy` or `join`, Spark shuffles data across the network. `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions` (default: 200) controls the post-shuffle partition count — override it per query with `spark.conf.set("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", "400")`. For large datasets, use `repartition(n, col("user_id"))` to co-locate related rows before a join, avoiding redundant shuffles. Use `coalesce(n)` when reducing partitions without a full shuffle.
bytes.code_snippet:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col

spark = SparkSession.builder.getOrCreate()

# Override shuffle partitions for this query
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", "400")

df = spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/events/")

# Partition pruning: Catalyst pushes this filter to file scan
filtered = df.filter(col("event_date") == "2024-01-15")

# Verify pruning in physical plan — look for PartitionFilters
filtered.explain(True)

# Repartition by join key to co-locate data before join
users = spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/users/")
result = df.repartition(400, col("user_id")).join(users, "user_id")

# Reduce partition count without full shuffle
result.coalesce(50).write.parquet("s3://bucket/output/")

bytes.topic: Incremental Processing — processing only new/changed data, watermarking
bytes.title: Spark Structured Streaming — Incremental Processing with Watermarks
bytes.body:
Spark Structured Streaming processes data incrementally by default — each trigger reads only new records from the source offset forward. The engine maintains offset tracking automatically for sources like Kafka, Delta Lake, and file-based streams. The real challenge is stateful aggregations: without bounds, state grows unboundedly across micro-batches.

Watermarking solves this. Call `.withWatermark("event_time", "10 minutes")` on your streaming DataFrame before a groupBy aggregation. Spark uses the watermark to track the max event time seen and drops state for windows older than `max_event_time - threshold`. This caps memory usage and lets the engine finalize late-arriving-data windows and emit results.

For Delta Lake sources, use `readStream` with `.option("readChangeFeed", "true")` on a CDF-enabled table to consume only row-level changes — inserts, updates, deletes — since the last checkpoint. Combined with `checkpointLocation`, Spark resumes exactly from the last committed offset on restart, making incremental pipelines fault-tolerant without manual offset management.
bytes.code_snippet:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import window, count

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("IncrementalDemo").getOrCreate()

kafka_df = (
    spark.readStream
    .format("kafka")
    .option("kafka.bootstrap.servers", "broker:9092")
    .option("subscribe", "events")
    .load()
)

agg_df = (
    kafka_df
    .withWatermark("timestamp", "10 minutes")
    .groupBy(window("timestamp", "5 minutes"))
    .agg(count("*").alias("event_count"))
)

(
    agg_df.writeStream
    .outputMode("append")  # only emit finalized windows
    .option("checkpointLocation", "/tmp/checkpoints/events")
    .format("delta")
    .start("/tmp/output/event_counts")
    .awaitTermination()
)

bytes.topic: Error Handling — dead letter queues, partial failure handling, alerting
bytes.title: Spark Structured Streaming — Dead Letter Queues and Partial Failure Handling
bytes.body:
Spark Structured Streaming has no built-in dead letter queue (DLQ), so you must implement one explicitly. The pattern: wrap your processing logic in a `try/catch` inside a `mapPartitions` or `flatMap`, emit failed records to a separate sink (Kafka topic, S3 path, or Delta table), and continue processing clean records.

Use `foreachBatch` to split a micro-batch into good and bad DataFrames before writing. This preserves exactly-once semantics on the success path while giving you full visibility into failures. Never silently drop bad records — schema mismatches, null key violations, and deserialization errors all surface here.

For alerting, attach a `StreamingQueryListener` and override `onQueryProgress` to emit custom metrics — record count, batch duration, input rows vs. output rows. Wire those metrics to Prometheus via a `CollectorRegistry` or push them to Grafana via StatsD. Set a Grafana alert if `numInputRows > 0 && numOutputRows == 0` for two consecutive batches — that pattern signals a silent partial failure where data is consumed but nothing is written.

bytes.topic: Testing Pipelines — unit testing transforms, integration testing end-to-end
bytes.title: Spark Testing Pipelines — Unit Testing Transforms and Integration Testing End-to-End
bytes.body:
Testing Spark pipelines requires two distinct strategies. For unit tests, use `SparkSession` in local mode with `master("local[*]")` to validate individual DataFrame transformations in isolation — no cluster needed. Wrap your transform logic in pure functions that accept and return DataFrames, making them trivially testable. Use `chispa`'s `assert_df_equality` (Python) or ScalaTest's `assertSmallDataFrameEquality` for schema-aware row comparison that handles column ordering and nullability correctly.

For integration tests, spin up a real pipeline end-to-end against a small fixture dataset written to a temp path using `spark.createDataFrame(...).write.parquet(tmp_path)`. Validate output schema with `df.schema == expectedSchema`, check row counts, and spot-check aggregations. Use `@pytest.fixture(scope="session")` to share one `SparkSession` across the test suite — creating a session per test adds 10–30 seconds of JVM startup overhead. Run integration tests with a Spark config that disables shuffle partitions: `spark.conf.set("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", "4")` to keep tests fast on small data.
bytes.code_snippet:
import pytest
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from chispa.dataframe_comparer import assert_df_equality
from my_pipeline.transforms import normalize_events

@pytest.fixture(scope="session")
def spark():
    return (SparkSession.builder
        .master("local[*]")
        .config("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", "4")
        .getOrCreate())

def test_normalize_events_drops_nulls(spark):
    raw = spark.createDataFrame(
        [(1, "click", None), (2, "view", "home")],
        ["user_id", "event", "page"]
    )
    result = normalize_events(raw)
    assert result.count() == 1
    assert "page" in result.columns

bytes.topic: Orchestration Patterns — fan-out/fan-in, dynamic task mapping, conditional branches
bytes.title: Spark Orchestration Patterns — Fan-Out/Fan-In, Dynamic Task Mapping, and Conditional Branches
bytes.body:
Spark's DAG execution model enables sophisticated orchestration without an external scheduler. Fan-out happens naturally when you call `flatMap` or `explode` to emit multiple output records per input, then chain downstream transformations — each partition processes independently. Fan-in uses `union`, `join`, or `reduceByKey` to collapse distributed results back into a unified dataset.

Dynamic task mapping in Spark means parameterizing jobs at runtime using input metadata. Read a manifest DataFrame listing S3 paths, then use `foreachPartition` or a loop over `collect()`'d configs to dispatch per-source read/transform pipelines. For true dynamic parallelism, pair this with `spark.sparkContext.parallelize(configs).flatMap(process)` so Spark schedules work across executors rather than the driver.

Conditional branches use DataFrame filter splits: `val hot = df.filter($"temp" > 90); val cold = df.filter($"temp" <= 90)`. Each branch materializes independently — cache the parent with `df.persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_AND_DISK)` before splitting to avoid recomputation. Use `df.explain(true)` to verify Spark isn't re-scanning the source twice when branch predicates are non-overlapping.
bytes.code_snippet:
val manifest = spark.read.json("s3://bucket/manifest/*.json")
val configs = manifest.collect().map(r => (r.getString(0), r.getString(1)))

val results = spark.sparkContext
  .parallelize(configs, numSlices = configs.length)
  .flatMap { case (path, schema) =>
    spark.read.schema(schema).parquet(path)
      .filter($"event_type" === "click")
      .toLocalIterator
  }

val unified = spark.createDataFrame(results, outputSchema)
unified.persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_AND_DISK)

val critical = unified.filter($"severity" === "P0")
val standard = unified.filter($"severity" =!= "P0")

critical.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("s3://bucket/critical/")
standard.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("s3://bucket/standard/")

bytes.topic: Performance — Spark shuffle optimization, broadcast joins, resource sizing
bytes.title: Spark Shuffle Optimization — Broadcasts, Partitions, and Sizing That Actually Matter
bytes.body:
Shuffles are Spark's most expensive operation — data crosses the network, spills to disk, and serializes repeatedly. Three levers matter most.

First, broadcast joins eliminate shuffles entirely for small tables. Set `spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold` (default 10MB) to match your driver memory headroom, or force it with `broadcast()`. If Catalyst picks a SortMergeJoin for a 50MB lookup table, you're paying shuffle tax unnecessarily.

Second, `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions` defaults to 200 — fine for 10GB, catastrophic for 10TB (too few partitions → OOM) or 100MB (too many → scheduling overhead). Target 100–200MB per partition post-shuffle. Use `df.rdd.getNumPartitions()` before and after transformations to confirm.

Third, executor sizing follows the 5-core rule: 5 cores per executor balances HDFS throughput (3–5 concurrent reads) without overwhelming GC. With 16-core nodes, run 3 executors of 5 cores each, leaving 1 core for the OS. Set `spark.executor.memoryOverhead` to at least 10% of executor memory to absorb off-heap usage from UDFs and Arrow-based pandas UDFs.
bytes.code_snippet:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast

spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .config("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", "400") \
    .config("spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold", "52428800") \
    .config("spark.executor.cores", "5") \
    .config("spark.executor.memoryOverhead", "512m") \
    .getOrCreate()

events = spark.table("events")          # large fact table
dim_device = spark.table("dim_device")  # small dimension ~40MB

# Force broadcast — bypasses SortMergeJoin, zero shuffle
result = events.join(broadcast(dim_device), "device_id")
result.explain()  # verify BroadcastHashJoin in physical plan

bytes.topic: Data Lineage — tracking data origins, impact analysis, documentation
bytes.title: Spark Data Lineage — Tracking Origins with DAGs, explain(), and OpenLineage
bytes.body:
Spark tracks transformation lineage natively through its DAG — every RDD holds a reference to its parent transformations. For DataFrames, `df.explain(mode="extended")` dumps four plan stages: parsed, analyzed, optimized, and physical. The analyzed plan is where column-level provenance lives — each output column maps to an `AttributeReference` tied back to its source relation.

For RDD pipelines, `rdd.toDebugString()` prints the full dependency tree with shuffle boundaries marked by indentation depth.

For production lineage, the OpenLineage Spark listener is the practical choice. Configure it via `spark.extraListeners` — no code changes to your jobs required. On every Spark action, it emits structured events (input datasets, output datasets, schema, job metadata) to a compatible backend like Marquez or DataHub.

Impact analysis becomes tractable once lineage is captured: query upstream/downstream graphs to identify all jobs reading a column before a schema change ships. Column-level lineage beyond `explain()` requires parsing `qe.analyzed().output()` inside a `QueryExecutionListener.onSuccess()` callback, or using the `spark-extension` library to extract `ColumnLineage` nodes directly from the logical plan.
bytes.code_snippet:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession

spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName("etl-pipeline") \
    .config("spark.extraListeners",
            "io.openlineage.spark.agent.OpenLineageSparkListener") \
    .config("spark.openlineage.transport.type", "http") \
    .config("spark.openlineage.transport.url", "http://marquez:5000") \
    .config("spark.openlineage.namespace", "data-platform") \
    .getOrCreate()

df = spark.read.parquet("s3://warehouse/sales/") \
           .filter("amount > 100") \
           .join(spark.read.parquet("s3://warehouse/customers/"), "customer_id")

df.explain(mode="extended")  # parsed → analyzed → optimized → physical

bytes.topic: Monitoring — pipeline SLAs, data freshness alerts, observability
bytes.title: Spark Pipeline SLAs — Monitoring Data Freshness with Structured Streaming Metrics
bytes.body:
Spark Structured Streaming exposes a rich set of built-in metrics through `StreamingQueryListener` that most teams underuse. Attach a listener to your `SparkSession` to capture `onQueryProgress` events — each fires after every micro-batch and contains `inputRowsPerSecond`, `processedRowsPerSecond`, `durationMs` (per stage), and crucially `sources[].endOffset` with event-time watermarks.

For data freshness SLAs, compare `sources[].description` event-time against wall-clock time. If your watermark lags by more than your SLA window (e.g., 15 minutes for near-real-time pipelines), emit an alert. Pair this with `queryProgress.batchDuration` to catch micro-batches exceeding your latency budget.

Send metrics to Prometheus via a custom `StreamingQueryListener` that pushes to a Pushgateway, or pipe `queryProgress` JSON to a Delta table for Grafana dashboarding. For batch jobs, use `SparkListener` and track `onJobEnd` stage metrics — `stageInfo.completionTime` minus `submissionTime` gives wall-clock stage SLA compliance directly. Never rely solely on Spark UI; it's ephemeral and not alertable.

bytes.topic: Infrastructure — cluster sizing, spot/preemptible instances, cost optimization
bytes.title: Spark Cluster Sizing on Spot Instances — Cut Costs Without Killing Jobs
bytes.body:
Running Spark on spot/preemptible instances can cut costs 60–80%, but naïve configs get you killed mid-stage. The key levers are dynamic allocation, speculation, and checkpointing.

Enable `spark.dynamicAllocation.enabled=true` with `spark.dynamicAllocation.shuffleTracking.enabled=true` — this lets Spark release idle executors without losing shuffle data. Set `spark.dynamicAllocation.executorIdleTimeout=60s` aggressively; unused executors bleed money.

For executor sizing, prefer fewer fat executors over many thin ones on spot: 4 cores / 16 GB each hits the sweet spot — enough parallelism per node while reducing scheduling overhead. Set `spark.executor.memoryOverhead` to at least 10% of heap to prevent OOM kills from JVM metaspace.

Enable `spark.speculation=true` with `spark.speculation.multiplier=1.5` and `spark.speculation.quantile=0.75` — this relaunches stragglers caused by spot preemptions before they stall your whole stage. Pin your driver to an on-demand node via node selectors or Databricks job cluster config. Losing the driver kills the entire job — executors are replaceable, the driver is not.

bytes.topic: Choosing an Orchestrator — Airflow vs Prefect vs Dagster decision framework
bytes.title: Spark Orchestration — Airflow vs Prefect vs Dagster Decision Framework
bytes.body:
The key axis is job dynamism vs. scheduling simplicity. Airflow's SparkSubmitOperator (or SparkKubernetesOperator on K8s) excels at static, scheduled batch pipelines — nightly ETL, daily aggregations. It breaks down on runtime parameterization; generating per-partition Spark jobs requires TaskGroup hacks and becomes unreadable fast.

Prefect fits when your flows need runtime branching — e.g., conditionally skipping a transformation stage based on a DataFrame.count() check from an upstream task. Its flow/task model wraps spark-submit calls without forcing static DAG topology, and concurrent Spark submissions are first-class.

Dagster's asset model aligns naturally with medallion architectures (Bronze → Silver → Gold). Declare each spark.table() write as a @asset with compute_kind='spark' — lineage, freshness policies, and partition-aware materialization are built in. dagster-pyspark provides a SparkSession resource injected directly into asset functions, keeping SparkSession lifecycle managed and testable.

Rule of thumb: Scheduled batch on YARN/EMR → Airflow. Dynamic branching at runtime → Prefect. Lakehouse lineage across medallion layers → Dagster.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Airflow: static batch job
from airflow.providers.apache.spark.operators.spark_submit import SparkSubmitOperator
submit_job = SparkSubmitOperator(
    task_id="silver_transform",
    application="/jobs/silver_transform.py",
    conf={"spark.executor.memory": "4g", "spark.executor.cores": "2"},
)

# Dagster: asset-based Spark with managed SparkSession
from dagster import asset
from dagster_pyspark import PySparkResource

@asset(compute_kind="spark", required_resource_keys={"pyspark"})
def silver_events(context):
    spark = context.resources.pyspark.spark_session
    return spark.table("bronze.events").filter("event_type IS NOT NULL") \
        .write.mode("overwrite").saveAsTable("silver.events")
